era_context: The Classical Greek world at a pivotal moment: the Battle of Leuctra in this year marks the end of Spartan hegemony and the beginning of Theban ascendancy. The Persian Empire under Artaxerxes II was stable but facing chronic satrapal challenges. In China, the Warring States period was intensifying, with major states consolidating power. Egypt under Nectanebo I was experiencing renewed independence and cultural flourishing.
geographic_coverage_gaps: Detailed records from Persian interior (beyond Greek sources)

events.title: Battle of Leuctra: Theban victory over Sparta
events.description: On a date traditionally placed in summer 371 BCE (exact date uncertain, possibly July), Epaminondas led a Theban and allied Boeotian army to a decisive victory over the Spartan hoplite phalanx near the village of Leuctra in Boeotia. The Theban force, numbering approximately 6,000–7,000 hoplites, defeated a Spartan army of similar size commanded by King Cleombrotus I. The battle was revolutionary in its tactics: Epaminondas deployed a deep phalanx on the left wing (the 'Sacred Band' of elite Theban warriors) while weakening his right, concentrating overwhelming force at the point of contact. This broke the myth of Spartan invincibility and marked the beginning of the end of Spartan hegemony in Greece. Cleombrotus I was killed in the battle.
events.certainty_note: The battle is attested in multiple contemporary and near-contemporary sources. The date is traditionally given as 371 BCE, though some scholars propose 370 BCE. The tactical details are well-documented, though some specifics (exact troop numbers, precise positioning) vary between sources.
cross_references: -362-001 (Battle of Mantinea, end of Theban hegemony)

events.title: Death of Cleombrotus I of Sparta
events.description: Cleombrotus I, one of the two kings of Sparta, was killed in the Battle of Leuctra. His death marked a symbolic end to Spartan military dominance and contributed to the political instability that would plague Sparta in subsequent decades. The succession passed to his son Agesilaus II, but the loss of prestige and military power was irreversible. Cleombrotus' death is often cited as the moment when Sparta's position as the leading Greek power definitively ended.
events.certainty_note: The death of Cleombrotus I at Leuctra is well-attested in ancient sources.

events.title: Theban Sacred Band emerges as elite military unit
events.description: The Sacred Band of Thebes, an elite unit of 300 hoplites composed of pairs of lovers (according to tradition), played a crucial role in the victory at Leuctra. While the unit may have existed earlier, it gained prominence and fame through this battle. The Sacred Band represented a new model of military organization based on personal bonds and intensive training rather than aristocratic birth. The unit would remain a formidable force in Greek warfare until its destruction at the Battle of Chaeronea in 338 BCE.
events.certainty_note: The existence and role of the Sacred Band at Leuctra is attested in ancient sources, though the details of its organization and the nature of the bonds between members are debated by modern scholars. Some sources may be romanticizing or projecting later ideals onto the unit.
cross_references: -338-001 (Battle of Chaeronea, destruction of Sacred Band)

events.title: Continued consolidation of Nectanebo I's rule in Egypt
events.description: Nectanebo I continued to consolidate his rule and expel remaining Persian influence from Egypt. He initiated major building projects, including temples and fortifications, as part of a broader program of cultural and economic renewal. The 30th Dynasty under Nectanebo represented a genuine renaissance of pharaonic power and Egyptian cultural identity after decades of Persian domination.
events.certainty_note: Nectanebo I's reign and building activities are well-documented in Egyptian sources and confirmed by archaeological evidence.

events.title: Artaxerxes II maintains Persian hegemony over Greek Asia Minor
events.description: Following the King's Peace of 387 BCE, Artaxerxes II maintained Persian control over the Greek city-states of the Ionian coast and other regions of Asia Minor. This arrangement was enforced through a combination of diplomatic pressure, military garrisons, and the support of local tyrants and oligarchs. The Greek cities remained nominally independent but were effectively under Persian suzerainty, a situation that would persist until the Macedonian conquests of Alexander the Great.
events.certainty_note: The continuation of Persian hegemony over Greek Asia Minor is well-attested in contemporary sources.

disconfirming_evidence: The exact date of the Battle of Leuctra is debated; some scholars place it in 370 BCE rather than 371 BCE. The traditional narrative of Spartan decline as sudden and catastrophic has been revised by modern scholarship, which emphasizes that Sparta remained a significant military power for decades after Leuctra and that the decline was more gradual. The romantic portrayal of the Sacred Band as a unit of lovers may be a later literary invention; contemporary sources are less explicit about this aspect. Some scholars question whether the Sacred Band was as large or as significant as later sources suggest.
historiographic_note: The Battle of Leuctra is one of the best-documented events of the Classical Greek period, attested in multiple contemporary and near-contemporary sources. However, the sources are primarily Greek and reflect Greek perspectives, particularly Athenian and Theban viewpoints. Persian sources are largely lost. The battle has been extensively analyzed by modern historians and archaeologists, but some details remain contested. The significance of the battle in terms of broader historical change (the 'decline of the polis,' the rise of Macedonia, etc.) is a matter of ongoing scholarly debate. The record is heavily weighted toward political and military events; economic and social dimensions are underrepresented.
